about.subtitle: ABOUT US
about.main_title: A LEGAL COMPANY HELPING BUSINESSES IN UZBEKISTAN OPERATE LEGALLY, EFFICIENTLY, AND WITHOUT UNNECESSARY COSTS.
about.desc_1: The main goals of our team are to achieve positive results, provide a high level of legal service, and prioritize the client's interests. We strive not just to meet, but to exceed our clients' expectations.
about.desc_2: Traffic Law successfully operates in the legal consulting and services market, providing comprehensive legal support to both local and international companies. Each year, the number of our clients and the range of our capabilities grow steadily.
about.desc_3: We provide legal support for business in all areas of activity, from the registration of legal entities to judicial representation, corporate disputes, and the protection of rights in administrative bodies. Our expertise covers civil, corporate, administrative, and family law.
about.quote: The company pays great attention to constantly improving the professional level of each lawyer by participating in specialized seminars, conferences, and advanced training courses. Representatives of our company actively participate in the legal community of Uzbekistan, speaking at legal forums, legislative seminars, and business conferences.

item1.title: Client Defense in Property Dispute
item1.task: A large construction company faced a claim for over 2 billion UZS and a 35% penalty for allegedly unfulfilled obligations.
item1.actions: Legal analysis of documentation was conducted, evidence was gathered, and a defense strategy was implemented in Tashkent Economic Court.
item1.result: The claim was fully dismissed. The client avoided losses of 2.7 billion UZS and maintained their business reputation.

item2.title: Appealing a Tax Committee Decision
item2.task: A trading company was charged 450 million UZS in taxes and penalties for allegedly improper use of tax benefits.
item2.actions: Procedural violations were identified, legal justification based on Tax Code was prepared, and the client was represented in appeals.
item2.result: The decision was fully overturned. The tax benefit status was maintained, saving the client 450 million UZS.

item3.title: Legal Support for Company Registration
item3.task: A foreign IT company faced difficulties understanding local legislation when opening a representative office.
item3.actions: A full package of JV LLC documents was prepared, registration in the Ministry of Justice was completed, and E-signatures were obtained.
item3.result: The company was registered within 10 business days, starting legal operations with full tax benefits.

items.title: Tax Code Changes 2026: What Should Businesses Prepare For?
items.desc: Starting Jan 1, 2026, new changes affect VAT for importers, income tax for IT companies, and mandatory electronic document flow.

content.lead: From 1 January 2026, a package of amendments to the Tax Code takes effect. The changes affect importers, the IT sector and the electronic document workflow. Below are the key points businesses should account for in advance.

sections.heading: 1. VAT for importers
sections.text: The rules for applying VAT rates and deductions on imported goods are being revised. Companies should reconcile commodity codes and update their accounting policy to avoid additional assessments.

sections.heading: 2. Corporate income tax for IT companies
sections.text: The conditions for preferential rates for IT Park residents are being clarified. Eligibility is now directly tied to the share of core revenue and proper separate accounting.

sections.heading: 3. Electronic document workflow
sections.text: The list of operations requiring mandatory electronic invoices is expanding. Paper equivalents will no longer be legally valid in these cases.

sections.heading: 4. Transition period
sections.text: A transition period applies to some requirements. It is important to record which rules apply to contracts concluded before the amendments took effect.

sections.heading: 5. What to do now
sections.text: Audit your contracts and accounting policy, update primary document templates and, if needed, obtain clarification on disputed operations before the reporting period begins.

content.conclusion: Timely preparation reduces the risk of additional assessments and penalties. We help run the audit and adapt your document workflow to the new requirements.

items.title: New Personal Data Protection Rules — A Business Guide
items.desc: Data protection liberalization: mandatory consent for processing, appointment of data officers, and fines up to 100 million UZS for leaks.

content.lead: Personal data legislation has been updated: requirements for consent, storage and liability are becoming stricter. Here is what it means for business and how to prepare.

sections.heading: 1. Mandatory consent to processing
sections.text: Consent must be specific, informed and verifiable. Generic wording such as "I agree to everything" no longer counts as a proper legal basis.

sections.heading: 2. Data protection officer
sections.text: Companies must appoint a person responsible for processing personal data and define their authority in an internal document.

sections.heading: 3. Localisation and storage
sections.text: Rules on storing citizens' data and cross-border transfers are clarified. You must verify where your databases and servers are physically located.

sections.heading: 4. Penalties for breaches
sections.text: Violations and leaks carry penalties up to significant amounts. Liability applies even in the absence of direct intent.

sections.heading: 5. How to prepare the company
sections.text: Update your privacy policy, consent forms and contractor agreements, and take an inventory of all data processing activities.

content.conclusion: Aligning processes with the new rules is an ongoing practice, not a one-off task. Our lawyers will help you build a compliant data processing system.

items.title: Maternity and Birth Benefits Increased in 2026
items.desc: Government allowances for pregnancy and birth have increased. Learn how to correctly manage HR documentation under the new labor rules.

content.lead: In 2026, the amounts of maternity and childcare benefits have been revised. Employers should correctly rebuild their HR records to avoid errors in calculations.

sections.heading: 1. What changed in benefit amounts
sections.text: Both lump-sum and monthly payments have increased. The calculation is tied to the current base amount at the date of the event.

sections.heading: 2. Lump-sum birth benefit
sections.text: The amount of the childbirth payment has been updated. Eligibility and the amount are fixed at the moment of application.

sections.heading: 3. Monthly benefits
sections.text: Monthly childcare benefits have been revised. Employers should apply the new coefficients when preparing payroll.

sections.heading: 4. HR records under the new rules
sections.text: Local acts, orders and payroll documents must be updated. Errors in paperwork directly affect the payable amounts.

sections.heading: 5. Common employer mistakes
sections.text: The most frequent are an incorrect calculation date, an incomplete document package and failure to notify the employee of due payments on time.

content.conclusion: Accurate HR records protect both the employee and the company. We advise on structuring payments and bringing documents in line with the new rules.
